title: Personal Expense Tracker
description: A command-line tool that helps users track daily expenses, categorize spending, and generate simple summary reports. Great for learning file handling, loops, and basic data processing.
roadmap: Step 1: Set up the project folder and create main.py
Step 2: Design the expense data structure as a dictionary
Step 3: Write functions to add and delete expenses
Step 4: Implement category filtering logic
Step 5: Write the summary report generator
Step 6: Add CSV export functionality
Step 7: Test with sample data and fix bugs

title: Weather Dashboard
description: A simple web page that fetches weather data from a free API and displays current conditions for any city. Teaches API calls, DOM manipulation, and basic UI design.
features: Show a weather icon based on conditions
roadmap: Step 1: Create the HTML structure with a search form
Step 2: Style the page with CSS flexbox
Step 3: Sign up for a free OpenWeatherMap API key
Step 4: Write the fetch() call to get weather data
Step 5: Parse the JSON response and extract key fields
Step 6: Display the data dynamically using DOM methods
Step 7: Add the Celsius/Fahrenheit toggle button

title: Student Grade Manager
description: A Python application to store student names and their grades, compute averages, and display a class report. Ideal for practicing data structures, functions, and file persistence.
features: Add students and assign grades per subject
Save and load data from a JSON file
roadmap: Step 1: Define the student data structure using a dictionary
Step 2: Write add_student() and add_grade() functions
Step 3: Implement average calculation logic
Step 4: Create a letter grade converter function
Step 5: Build the JSON save/load functions
Step 6: Create a simple text menu for user interaction
Step 7: Write a class report printer function

title: Task Manager REST API
description: A RESTful API built with Flask that allows clients to create, read, update, and delete tasks. Perfect for learning API design, HTTP methods, and JSON responses.
features: Persist data to a JSON file
roadmap: Step 1: Install Flask and create the app.py file
Step 2: Define the task data model as a dictionary
Step 3: Create the GET /tasks endpoint to list all tasks
Step 4: Create the POST /tasks endpoint to add a task
Step 5: Create PUT /tasks/<id> to update a task
Step 6: Create DELETE /tasks/<id> to remove a task
Step 7: Add JSON file persistence for saving tasks
Step 8: Test all endpoints using Postman or curl

title: Portfolio Website
description: A personal portfolio site with sections for bio, projects, and contact. A great first project that teaches HTML layout, CSS styling, and a bit of JavaScript for interactivity.
features: Hero section with name and tagline
roadmap: Step 1: Plan the page sections on paper first
Step 2: Write the HTML structure for all sections
Step 3: Add CSS reset and base typography styles
Step 4: Style the navigation and hero section
Step 5: Build the projects grid using CSS Grid
Step 6: Add the contact form with labels and inputs
Step 7: Write JavaScript for form validation
Step 8: Make the site responsive with media queries

title: URL Shortener
description: A full-stack web app that takes long URLs and generates short codes. Users can paste a link and get a shorter one back. Teaches Flask routing, random code generation, and front-end form handling.
features: Redirect short codes to original URL
Track how many times a link was clicked
List all shortened links in a dashboard
roadmap: Step 1: Set up Flask app with two routes: home and redirect
Step 2: Write a random 6-character code generator
Step 3: Store URL mappings in a JSON file
Step 4: Build the HTML form for pasting long URLs
Step 5: Display the shortened URL after submission
Step 6: Implement the redirect route using the short code
Step 7: Add a click counter that updates on each visit
Step 8: Build a simple dashboard to list all links

title: Data Analysis Report Generator
description: Upload a CSV file and automatically generate a summary report with statistics, missing value counts, and basic charts. A practical project for learning data wrangling and pandas.
features: Show column types and null counts
Calculate mean, median, and mode per column
Generate bar charts for categorical data
roadmap: Step 1: Install pandas and matplotlib via pip
Step 2: Write a CSV loader that validates the file path
Step 3: Generate a summary table of column info
Step 4: Compute descriptive statistics for numeric columns
Step 5: Count and display missing values per column
Step 6: Build chart generation functions using matplotlib
Step 7: Export the full report to a text or HTML file

title: Password Strength Checker
description: A tool that checks password strength based on length, symbols, uppercase letters, and numbers. Helps beginners understand input validation and security basics.
roadmap: Step 1: Create the password input system
Step 2: Check password length
Step 3: Detect uppercase and lowercase letters
Step 4: Detect numbers and symbols
Step 5: Create a scoring system
Step 6: Display password strength feedback

title: CLI Task Automation Tool
description: A command-line tool built in Go that automates repetitive file and folder management tasks. Great for learning Go's standard library, file I/O, and CLI argument parsing.
features: Organize files into folders by extension
Log all operations to a text file
roadmap: Step 1: Set up Go module and main.go entry point
Step 2: Parse CLI arguments using the flag package
Step 3: Write a function to scan a directory
Step 4: Implement file grouping by extension
Step 5: Write the batch rename logic
Step 6: Add empty folder cleanup function
Step 7: Implement operation logging to a file
Step 8: Test with sample directories

title: Student Quiz App
description: A console-based quiz application built in Kotlin that loads questions, tracks scores, and shows a final result. Perfect for learning Kotlin syntax, data classes, and control flow.
features: Load questions from a JSON file
Show final score and correct answers
roadmap: Step 1: Set up Kotlin project with main.kt
Step 2: Define a Question data class
Step 3: Load questions from a JSON file
Step 4: Display questions one by one in console
Step 5: Accept and validate user answers
Step 6: Track score with a counter variable
Step 7: Display final results with correct answers

title: Number Guessing Game
description: A classic number guessing game built in Rust. The program picks a random number and the user guesses until they get it right. Perfect for learning Rust ownership, input handling, and control flow.
features: Generate a random number in a range
roadmap: Step 1: Set up Rust project with cargo new
Step 2: Add rand crate to Cargo.toml
Step 3: Generate a random number using rand
Step 4: Read user input from stdin
Step 5: Parse input string to integer
Step 6: Compare guess with target and give hints
Step 7: Loop until correct guess and show attempts

title: Student Grade Calculator
description: A Java console app that takes student marks as input, calculates averages, and assigns letter grades. Great for learning Java basics like classes, loops, arrays, and methods.
roadmap: Step 1: Create a Student class with name and marks fields
Step 2: Write a method to calculate average
Step 3: Write a method to assign letter grade
Step 4: Use Scanner to take input from user
Step 5: Store multiple subject marks in an array
Step 6: Display the final result summary

title: Personal Finance Dashboard
description: A web-based personal finance tracker built with TypeScript. Users can add income and expenses, view summaries, and see charts. Teaches TypeScript types, interfaces, and DOM manipulation.
roadmap: Step 1: Set up TypeScript project with tsconfig.json
Step 2: Define Transaction interface with type, amount, category
Step 3: Write functions to add and delete transactions
Step 4: Calculate and display total balance
Step 5: Build the HTML form for adding entries
Step 6: Render transactions as a list in the DOM
Step 7: Add Chart.js pie chart for spending breakdown
Step 8: Add localStorage to persist data

title: File Compression Tool
description: A command-line file compression tool built in C++ that implements basic run-length encoding. Great for learning C++ file streams, string manipulation, and algorithm implementation.
features: Compress a text file using run-length encoding
Handle edge cases like empty files
roadmap: Step 1: Set up C++ project with main.cpp
Step 2: Write a file reader using fstream
Step 3: Implement run-length encoding algorithm
Step 4: Write compressed output to a new file
Step 5: Implement the decompression function
Step 6: Calculate and display compression ratio
Step 7: Handle edge cases and test with sample files

title: Personal Blog REST API
description: A simple REST API built in Go that handles blog posts. Users can create, read, update, and delete blog posts. Perfect for learning Go's net/http package, JSON encoding, and basic routing.
features: List all posts or get a single post
Store posts in memory as a map
Return JSON responses for all endpoints
roadmap: Step 1: Set up Go module and create main.go
Step 2: Define a BlogPost struct with id, title, and content
Step 3: Create an in-memory map to store posts
Step 4: Write a handler for GET /posts to list all posts
Step 5: Write a handler for POST /posts to create a post
Step 6: Write a handler for DELETE /posts/{id}
Step 7: Return proper JSON responses with status codes
Step 8: Test all endpoints using curl or Postman

title: File Organizer Automation
description: A Kotlin command-line tool that automatically organizes files in a folder by type, date, or size. Great for learning Kotlin file handling, extension functions, and collections.
features: Scan a folder and list all files
Group files by extension into subfolders
Move files automatically to correct folders
Generate a summary report of what was moved
roadmap: Step 1: Set up Kotlin project with main.kt
Step 2: Write a function to scan and list all files in a folder
Step 3: Group files by their extension using groupBy()
Step 4: Create subfolders for each file type
Step 5: Move files into correct subfolders
Step 6: Count moved files per category
Step 7: Print a summary report to the console

title: CSV Data Analyzer
description: A command-line tool in Rust that reads a CSV file and generates basic statistics like min, max, mean, and count per column. Great for learning Rust file I/O, error handling, and the csv crate.
features: Read and parse a CSV file
Calculate min, max, and mean per numeric column
Count total rows and missing values
Display a formatted summary table in terminal
roadmap: Step 1: Set up Rust project with cargo new
Step 2: Add csv and serde crates to Cargo.toml
Step 3: Write a CSV reader using the csv crate
Step 4: Parse each row into a vector of strings
Step 5: Detect numeric columns and parse values
Step 6: Calculate min, max, and mean for each column
Step 7: Count missing or empty values per column
Step 8: Display results as a formatted table

title: Student Registration System
description: A Java web application using Spring Boot that allows students to register, view, and delete their profiles. Great for learning Spring Boot basics, REST APIs, and Java annotations.
features: Register a new student with name and email
roadmap: Step 1: Create a Spring Boot project using Spring Initializr
Step 2: Define a Student class with id, name, and email fields
Step 3: Create a StudentController with REST endpoints
Step 4: Implement GET /students to list all students
Step 5: Implement POST /students to register a new student
Step 6: Implement DELETE /students/{id} to remove a student
Step 7: Add basic input validation using annotations
Step 8: Test endpoints using Postman

title: Memory Card Game
description: A browser-based memory card matching game built with TypeScript. Players flip cards to find matching pairs. Great for learning TypeScript basics, DOM manipulation, and game logic.
features: Display a grid of face-down cards
Flip two cards on click and check for a match
Show a win message when all pairs are found
roadmap: Step 1: Set up TypeScript project with tsconfig.json
Step 2: Define a Card interface with id, value, and isFlipped
Step 3: Create and shuffle the card array
Step 4: Render cards as a grid in the DOM
Step 5: Handle card click events and flip logic
Step 6: Compare two flipped cards for a match
Step 7: Track attempts counter and update display
Step 8: Detect win condition and show message

title: Simple Calculator
description: A console-based calculator in C++ that performs basic arithmetic operations. Perfect for absolute beginners learning C++ syntax, functions, switch statements, and user input handling.
features: Add, subtract, multiply, and divide two numbers
Display clear results with proper formatting
roadmap: Step 1: Create main.cpp and set up basic structure
Step 2: Write individual functions for each operation
Step 3: Display a menu for operation selection
Step 4: Accept two numbers as user input
Step 5: Use a switch statement to call the right function
Step 6: Handle division by zero with an if check
Step 7: Wrap everything in a loop for repeated use
